basic_meditation.title: Meditation Tutorial
basic_meditation.subtitle: How to actually sit — a short guide
basic_meditation.description: Nine short sections. Enough to take you through your first real sit on your own. Open it any time — no progress to worry about.

steps.title: Before we begin
steps.body:
This is a short guide to sitting meditation — enough to take you through your first real sit on your own. It will take about ten minutes to read, and then five to twenty minutes to actually do.

A few honest things first. You are not going to be "good at" meditation at the start, and that is not the point. The goal of this sit is not calm, not bliss, not emptiness, not any specific experience at all. The goal is to sit down, pay attention to the breath for a set amount of time, and watch what the mind does. That's the whole thing. Everything else — the stillness, the insight, the transformation — grows out of that one simple act done many times.

The Buddha taught this practice to complete beginners 2,500 years ago. It still works. Nothing here is secret, nothing requires special equipment, and you already have everything you need.

steps.title: Before you sit
steps.body:
Pick a time. Early morning is traditional because the mind is quieter, but any time works — the best time is the one you will actually do. Pick a length. For your first sit, five minutes is enough. You can always do more tomorrow. Set a timer on your phone and face the screen down so notifications don't reach you.

Pick a place. Any quiet spot. A corner of a room, a chair by a window, the edge of a bed. It does not need to be special. It should be somewhere you can come back to.

One practical note: go to the bathroom first, and don't sit immediately after a heavy meal. Both of those sabotage first sits for obvious reasons.

steps.title: Posture
steps.body:
You have three good options:

A chair — feet flat on the floor, back not touching the back of the chair (so the spine holds itself), hands resting on your thighs. This is the normal option for most people in the West. There is nothing lesser about it.

A cushion on the floor — sit on the edge of a firm cushion (or a folded blanket) high enough that your knees are below your hips. Legs crossed loosely in front of you. This is only comfortable if your body already allows it; don't force it.

The floor on your knees — kneeling with a cushion or small bench between your heels. Works well for some people, hurts others.

In all three: let the spine be straight but not stiff, the way a growing plant is straight. Shoulders relaxed — let them drop. Hands resting in your lap, palms up or down, whatever's easy. Eyes gently closed, or half-open looking down at a spot a meter in front of you.

If something starts to hurt during the sit, you are allowed to adjust. Do it slowly, deliberately, and return to the breath.

steps.title: A simple starting method — following the breath
steps.body:
The Buddha's own instructions on mindfulness of breathing (MN 10, MN 118) do not ask you to count. They ask you to know a long breath as long, a short breath as short, to feel the whole breath body, and gradually to let the breath calm itself. That is the canonical practice, and it is what you will eventually return to.

For your very first sits, however, many teachers offer counting as a temporary scaffold — not because the Buddha taught it, but because a complete beginner's mind has nothing to hold onto, and a number gives it something. Use it if it helps. Drop it the moment it stops helping.

When the timer starts, close your eyes. Take three slightly deeper breaths to settle. Then let the breathing become natural — don't control it, don't deepen it, don't time it. Just let the body breathe the way it already knows how.

If you want to try the scaffold: silently note "one" as you breathe in, "one" as you breathe out, then "two" / "two," up to ten, then restart. If it feels artificial, skip it — just feel the breath where it is most clearly felt in the body (the tip of the nose, the upper lip, the belly rising and falling) and stay there. Either approach is fine for the first weeks.

One detail either way: pick one specific place in the body where you can feel the breath most clearly, and stay with that one place for the whole sit. Don't keep switching, or you'll end up watching for the breath instead of feeling it.

steps.title: When the mind wanders
steps.body:
The mind will wander. This is not a bug. This is the entire thing you are training.

Within about fifteen seconds of starting, you will notice you have stopped counting and are thinking about something else — a conversation, a plan, a memory, a song. This has already happened to you many times today; you just didn't notice because there was no quiet place to notice from.

Here is the instruction, which is very important: when you notice you have wandered, do not be discouraged, do not criticize yourself, do not analyze what the thought was. Simply, gently, return to the breath and restart the count at one. That moment of noticing and returning is not a failure of meditation. It is the whole practice. Every time you notice and return, you are strengthening exactly the muscle that meditation builds — the muscle that will, over years, become the ability to be aware of what is happening while it is happening.

If you wander a hundred times in five minutes and come back a hundred times, you have had a perfect sit. The scoreboard is "did you come back," not "did you stay."

steps.title: Concentration and mindfulness
steps.body:
The tradition talks about two wings of practice. One is samatha — concentration, or calm-abiding. That's what counting the breath develops: the ability to keep attention on one thing. The other is vipassanā — insight, or clear seeing. That's the ability to observe what is actually happening in the body and mind right now, without adding or subtracting.

You do not need to choose between them. At the start, counting the breath develops both at once: the counting trains concentration, and the noticing-and-returning trains mindfulness.

Later, as your concentration gets steadier, you can drop the counting and simply feel the breath. Later still, you can let the breath recede and observe whatever is most prominent — a sensation in the body, a mood, the arising and passing of thought itself. But all of that grows out of the one practice you are doing today. There is no rush.

steps.title: How to end a sit
steps.body:
When the timer goes off, don't leap up. Take three slow breaths with the eyes still closed. Notice the state of your mind and body right now — you don't need to evaluate it, just see what's here. Then slowly open the eyes and look at the room as if for the first time.

The Tibetan teachers call the transition out of a sit "bringing the practice into life." The minute after the sit is the most important minute for the practice — what you do next, how you move, how you speak to the first person you see, is where the sit actually lives. Give yourself ten seconds before checking your phone.

That's one sit. That's it. You have now meditated.

steps.title: What to expect, and what not to expect
steps.body:
Some sits feel calm. Most don't, especially at the start. Some will feel like a storm of thoughts with no space in between; some will feel like boredom; some will feel like physical restlessness; a few will feel like nothing at all. All of those are normal, and none of them are a sign that it's not working.

The practice is not measured by how any individual sit felt. It is measured by whether you showed up, and by what slowly changes in your life over weeks and months — whether you snap at people less, whether you notice your own emotions sooner, whether you can choose your response instead of reacting, whether there is a little more space around your thoughts. Those are the real signs.

The Buddha compared it to the handle of a carpenter's adze (SN 22.101): you cannot see the wood wearing down day by day, but after long use, the shape of your hand is worn into it.

steps.title: Where to go from here
steps.body:
The single most useful thing you can do is sit again tomorrow. Short is fine — five to ten minutes — but daily. Consistency matters far more than duration at the start. Adze will help you keep that streak.

When five minutes feels stable, grow to ten. When ten feels stable, grow to fifteen or twenty. Don't skip ahead. The Pali suttas have a word for this: anupubbasikkhā — gradual training. The Buddha's own method.

Beyond that, here's the path most teachers suggest for the first year:
• Start with the sit you just learned — counted breath, daily.
• Once it feels steady, add metta (loving-kindness) as a second practice: two minutes of silently wishing wellbeing for yourself, a friend, a neutral person, a difficult person.
• Read one sutta a week. MN 10 (Satipaṭṭhāna) is the classical starter.
• Find a real community or teacher when you can. The sangha is one of the three jewels for a reason.

You now have everything you need to begin. Close this tab, set a timer for five minutes, and go sit.

metta_practice.title: Mettā — The Practice of Loving-Kindness
metta_practice.subtitle: The brahmavihāra that softens the heart
metta_practice.description: Seven sections on mettā — what it is, why the Buddha praised it so highly, the phrases, how to extend it from yourself outward, and what to do when it feels false. Best approached after you have a basic sit established.

steps.title: What metta is
steps.body:
Mettā is not affection, not romantic love, not sympathy. It is the active wish that beings be well — including beings you find difficult, and including yourself. The Buddha placed it among the four brahmavihāras, the "divine dwellings" — alongside compassion (karuṇā), sympathetic joy (muditā), and equanimity (upekkhā). These four are called divine because a heart that abides in them is, for as long as it does, free from the small prison of self-concern.

Mettā softens what concentration alone cannot reach: the heart that has been hardened by self-protection, by judgment, by comparison, by quiet hatred. Concentration produces clarity. Mettā produces warmth. The path needs both wings.

steps.title: Why mettā — its place in the path
steps.body:
In MN 21 (the Kakacūpama Sutta — the Simile of the Saw) the Buddha gave perhaps the most striking instruction in the entire canon. He said: "Even if bandits were to sever you savagely limb by limb with a two-handled saw, anyone who gave rise to a mind of hate toward them would not be carrying out my teaching. Here you should train: 'Our minds will be unaffected, and we shall utter no evil words; we shall abide compassionate for their welfare, with a mind of loving-kindness.'"

This is not sentimentality and it is not a counsel of passivity. It is a description of a mind that no longer needs the world to behave a particular way in order to remain at ease. That is the mind the path is building. Mettā is how that mind is trained — not by waiting for circumstances to be favorable, but by deliberately practicing the wish for wellbeing of all beings, including the ones who hurt you.

The Buddha said in AN 11.16 that one who develops mettā for even one moment is "not empty of jhāna," is loved by humans and non-humans alike, sleeps without bad dreams, and is dear to those who see them. These are practical effects, not metaphors.

steps.title: The phrases
steps.body:
The traditional Theravāda phrases are four:

  May this being be free from danger.
  May this being be free from mental suffering.
  May this being be free from physical suffering.
  May this being live with ease.

You can use these, or simpler ones — "may you be safe, may you be happy, may you be healthy, may you be at ease" — or your own words. The phrases are scaffolding. What matters is the wish underneath them and the steady attention to the being you're directing them toward.

Some teachers, including Sharon Salzberg in the modern Insight tradition, recommend keeping the same set of phrases for months at a time. Switching them constantly is a way the restless mind avoids actually doing the practice. Pick four and stay with them.

steps.title: Direction one — yourself
steps.body:
Begin with yourself. Many practitioners try to skip this step out of false modesty, or because directing kindness inward feels selfish. It is neither.

The Buddha addressed this directly in Ud 5.1 (the Rāja Sutta — King Pasenadi's discovery). The king and his queen, after looking inward, both reported that they could find no one dearer to themselves than themselves. They came to the Buddha embarrassed. The Buddha said: "Searching all directions with the mind, one finds no one dearer than oneself. So too is everyone else dear to themselves. Therefore one who loves the self should not harm another."

This is not narcissism. It is the foundation. If you cannot honestly wish wellbeing for yourself, the wish you extend to others will be a performance, and the heart will know it.

So: sit, settle, and bring yourself to mind clearly. Your body. Your day. Your tiredness or your steadiness, whatever is true. Then silently:

  May I be free from danger.
  May I be free from mental suffering.
  May I be free from physical suffering.
  May I live with ease.

Repeat slowly, three or four times. Don't try to feel anything in particular. Don't grade what arises. Just say the phrases and notice. If resistance comes — "I don't deserve this" or "this is silly" — let it come, and say the phrases again anyway.

steps.title: Widening the circle
steps.body:
When some warmth toward yourself is present — even a faint warmth is enough; you don't need to wait for bliss — extend the practice outward in the traditional sequence:

**An easy person.** Someone you find easy to love. A parent who treated you well, a child, a dear friend, a teacher who shaped you. Bring them clearly to mind. Send them the same phrases. Stay with them for a minute or two.

**A neutral person.** Someone you encounter regularly but have no strong feelings about — a cashier at the shop you visit, a neighbor you nod to, a colleague you barely know. The point of this stage is profound: most beings in your life are in this category, and the heart usually has no template for wishing them well. Practice creates one.

**A difficult person.** Someone you find hard. Don't start with your worst enemy on day one — start with someone mildly irritating, the way the Buddha would have a beginner approach this. Same phrases. Notice the resistance. Notice it without making a story about it.

If "may they live with ease" feels actively false for the difficult person, drop down to a lighter form: "may they be free from the anger that is hurting them," or "may they find what they truly need." Those versions are honest in a way the standard ones may not yet be, and they slip past the resistance instead of fighting it. The lighter form still works. Don't force what isn't there.

steps.title: When mettā feels false
steps.body:
It will, sometimes — and especially in the early weeks. The phrases will sound mechanical. The heart will feel numb. The difficult person will trigger plain irritation rather than any warmth. This is normal, and it is not a failure of the practice or of you.

Mettā cannot be forced. It can only be inclined toward, the way you incline a flame toward a wick rather than ramming it. When the practice feels hollow, do less, not more. Go back to yourself, or to the easy person, and rebuild the warmth there before extending it. Five minutes of honest mettā — even hesitant, faltering mettā — is worth far more than thirty minutes of grimly-recited phrases. The Buddha's instruction in AN 11.16 is that mettā is to be "developed, cultivated, and made much of like a vehicle." A vehicle works when its driver is willing, not when the driver is exhausted.

Two further notes. First, do not begin mettā practice with a romantic interest as the "easy person" — the practice gets crossed with desire and produces a confused state that isn't mettā at all. Use someone you love non-romantically. Second, do not try to send mettā to someone who has caused you serious harm if the wound is still raw — the practice can re-traumatize. Wait. The Buddha never said the path was a forced march.

steps.title: Closing the practice
steps.body:
End the practice by extending mettā in all directions — the traditional final movement:

  May all beings in the east be free from danger and live with ease.
  May all beings in the south, in the west, in the north,
  above and below, in all directions, near and far, seen and unseen,
  born and yet to be born — may all beings live with ease.

Sit for a minute in silence afterward. Don't analyze the experience. Just notice the state of the heart now, compared to when you sat down. Whatever you find is the data. Some sits will feel transformative; most will feel like ordinary sits with a slightly softer texture. Both are the practice working.

Then open your eyes slowly and bring whatever warmth is here into the next thing you do — the next person you meet, the next email you write, the next time someone irritates you. The minute after the sit is where the practice actually lives. The Buddha said in AN 8.1 that mettā has eleven benefits — sleeping at ease, waking at ease, no bad dreams, being loved by humans, being loved by non-humans, being protected by devas, fire and poison and weapons not affecting one, the mind concentrating quickly, the face becoming serene, dying without confusion, and (if not yet liberated) reappearing in the brahma world. Take that list as you wish. Some of it is observable in weeks. Some of it is the kind of claim only practice can verify.

jhana_intro.title: Jhāna — The Gathered Mind
jhana_intro.subtitle: An honest introduction to deep concentration
jhana_intro.description: Six sections on the jhānas — what they are, what they aren't, access concentration, the first jhāna, why the pleasure isn't craving, and three honest cautions before you try. Gated to Anāgāmi level and above because the early stages of practice need other things first.

steps.title: What jhāna is — and what it isn't
steps.body:
Jhāna is a state of deep meditative absorption described by the Buddha in MN 36, MN 39, AN 5.28, and many other suttas. Traditionally there are four jhānas (sometimes eight, when the formless attainments are added), each more refined than the last. The first jhāna is characterized by five factors: applied thought (vitakka), sustained thought (vicāra), rapture (pīti), happiness (sukha), and one-pointedness (ekaggatā). It is not enlightenment. It is not the goal of the path. It is a tool the Buddha praised highly because the mind in jhāna is unified, pliable, luminous, and stable enough that wisdom can do its real work afterward.

The Buddha himself entered jhāna on the night of his awakening (MN 36, the Mahāsaccaka Sutta). After six years of severe asceticism that nearly killed him, he remembered a moment from childhood when, sitting under a rose-apple tree while his father worked, he had spontaneously entered the first jhāna — without austerity, without strain, simply by being present. He realized this state was not to be feared. He ate, regained strength, and that night used the unified mind of the four jhānas as the platform from which he turned the attention to the three knowledges that ended his ignorance.

So: jhāna is not the awakening. Jhāna is what the Buddha used to make awakening possible. The distinction matters.

steps.title: Access concentration — the gate
steps.body:
Before any jhāna there is access concentration (upacāra-samādhi). This is the threshold state where the breath has become very refined, the five hindrances are temporarily suspended (not eliminated, just quiet), and the mind is gathered enough that a meditation sign — a nimitta — may begin to appear. The nimitta is often described as an inner light, a steady warm feeling, or a sense of the body becoming insubstantial. Different practitioners experience it differently; the form does not matter, the steadiness does.

Most practitioners — including most of the people you have ever met who meditate seriously — spend years at access concentration before any formal jhāna arises. And that is fine. Access concentration is itself a form of unified mind that the world rarely offers, and it is more than enough to ground real insight practice. The Visuddhimagga (the major Theravāda commentary) explicitly says that vipassanā can be done from access concentration alone, without ever entering the formal jhānas. Many serious practitioners and lineages — the entire dry-insight tradition, much of the modern Burmese vipassanā schools, large parts of the Thai forest tradition — proceed exactly this way.

So if you are reading this section: do not feel that something is wrong if jhāna does not arise. The threshold is the gift.

steps.title: The first jhāna
steps.body:
When access concentration matures and the conditions are right, joy and ease begin to fill the body, thinking quiets but does not yet vanish entirely, and attention becomes effortlessly absorbed in the breath or in the nimitta. The Buddha described it (MN 39, MN 119, and many others) as: "Quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unwholesome states, one enters and abides in the first jhāna, which is accompanied by applied and sustained thought, with rapture and happiness born of seclusion."

The crucial verb is "enters" — not "produces," not "achieves." You do not enter the first jhāna by trying. Trying breaks it; the very effort is a movement of the discursive mind, and discursive movement is what jhāna is the absence of. You enter the first jhāna by gathering the mind, settling at access, letting the gathered mind tip in on its own.

The taste of it, when it does happen, is unmistakable. The body fills with a kind of pleasant pressure or current that has nothing to do with sense pleasure. Joy arises that is not joy about anything. Time feels suspended. When the sit ends, the afterglow can last for hours.

Do not chase the afterglow. That is how jhāna becomes its own attachment.

steps.title: Why pleasure here isn't craving
steps.body:
A reasonable concern: isn't pursuing the bliss of jhāna just another form of grasping? Doesn't the Buddha's whole teaching point away from craving for pleasant experience?

He addressed this directly in MN 139 (the Araṇavibhaṅga Sutta — the Exposition of Non-Conflict). He distinguished two kinds of pleasure: sensual pleasure, which is to be feared and avoided because it inflames craving and binds, and the pleasure of seclusion (which includes the jhānic factors of pīti and sukha), which is to be cultivated, developed, and pursued. He said directly: "The pleasure of seclusion is to be pursued, developed, and cultivated. It is not to be feared."

A mind that has tasted the joy of unification finds the pull of sense pleasures progressively less convincing — not because of moral effort but because the sense pleasures simply do not compare. This is one of the structural reasons jhāna helps the path: it loosens craving from the inside, by offering the mind something the world cannot offer back. Pīti and sukha are not goals to grasp but evidence that practice is working.

The actual danger is different from what beginners fear. The danger is not that you will enjoy the pleasure too much. The danger is that you will enjoy it so much you stop using the gathered mind for the wisdom work it was meant for, and start collecting jhāna experiences as a hobby. The Buddha rejected his teachers Āḷāra Kālāma and Uddaka Rāmaputta for exactly this — they could enter the formless attainments but had not used the unified mind for liberation (MN 26).

steps.title: Three honest cautions
steps.body:
Before you try, three warnings.

**First: jhāna is rare.** Not because the path is broken, but because the conditions for jhāna require sustained seclusion, sīla so clean that no remorse troubles the mind, and far more concentration than most modern practitioners can build between work, family, and a phone in their pocket. Most serious practitioners — including monastics — never enter the formal jhānas. They walk the entire path on access concentration plus insight, and they wake up. There is nothing wrong with this. If you spend a decade trying to build jhāna and the result is just a steadier attention, that decade was not wasted.

**Second: what people call "jhāna" varies enormously.** From the genuinely deep absorptions described in the Visuddhimagga (which leave the practitioner unable to hear sounds and require minutes to "exit") to "lite jhāna" or "sutta jhāna" approaches that emphasize the joy and ease without requiring the full absorption. There is real disagreement among modern teachers about which of these counts. Don't get pulled into the controversy. Practice what is in front of you.

**Third: jhāna can become its own attachment.** The "jhāna junkie" is a real phenomenon. The Buddha was clear that the jhānas are vehicles, not destinations. Use the gathered mind for what it was meant for: turning the attention to impermanence, suffering, and not-self in your own experience. If you find yourself sitting only to chase the bliss, you have stopped practicing the dhamma and started practicing pleasure-seeking with extra steps.

steps.title: How to begin (briefly)
steps.body:
If you want to try, the traditional entry is through the breath, via the same ānāpānasati practice you already do — there is no separate technique. As your concentration deepens over weeks and months, the breath becomes very subtle, sometimes seeming to almost disappear. A sense of refined pleasure may begin to spread through the body. Don't grasp at it; let it grow on its own. Don't analyze it; analysis is a movement of the discursive mind, and discursive movement is what you are leaving behind. Stay with the simplicity of breath-awareness, and let the unification happen on its own schedule, in its own time.

If a steady inner light or a warm bodily feeling appears, gently soften the attention onto it instead of the breath. Don't stare at it; rest in it. If the attention slips back to the breath, fine — that's also working. If nothing happens, that's also fine — return tomorrow.

Three practical recommendations. First, do this on retreat or in long sits, not in 20-minute sessions; the conditions for jhāna require sustained seclusion the workday cannot give you. Second, do not read internet accounts of other people's jhāna experiences while you are practicing — they will become a template the mind tries to match, and matching is grasping. Third, find a teacher who actually has experience with these states if you can. The Thai forest tradition (Ajahn Brahm in particular has written extensively and accessibly on jhāna), the Pa Auk Sayadaw lineage in Burma, and the Bhikkhu Anālayo lectures are good places to look.

The Buddha trained for six years before entering jhāna on the night of his awakening. Patience is not a virtue here — it is the practice itself.
